comments.text: I've run it multiple times over with a GPT-5.4 Pro instance and every time AI seems to be confused by the comment here below the problem, especially $k=1,2$ case. It seems Erdos also meant a weaker statement for almost all integers (density 1) rather than sufficiently large. I attach the note which shows both strong and weak version IF one know case of $k=1$ (which is noted in the comments as true, but I couldn't find it anywhere), and prove that $k=1$ for what is in the Erdos-Graham paper, and argues that the strong version shouldnt be true in general. I'd love a second opinion of whether the statement should be corrected and to what - ideally checked with a precise Erdos reference, because I don't see a reference here (have one in my note if that's what meant).
For this one I'll wait for more clarification before checking.

The problem is mentioned in page 29 of OLD AND NEW PROBLEMS AND RESULTS IN COMBINATORIAL NUMBER THEORY . The question is asked for all sufficiently large integers, but there is no claim that a solution is known when $k=1$ or $2$.

I agree that the final sentence should be changed to something like: For all $k$, any set $A$ such that $\sum_{n\in A}\frac{1}{n}=\infty$ has the property that almost all integers can be written this way. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
Yes, this comment would be much better. And I think the main difficulty is actually proving it for either $k=1,2$ or $3$. The rest follows from the 'lift' argument in my note.

Well, $a_p = c$ for all $p$ works for $k = 1$ and all $c$. I don't immediately see a solution for $k = 2$, but $a_2 = 1$ and $a_p = 0$ for $p \ge 3$ already brings you pretty close, as then you're only missing powers of two.
Thanks, I agree, I think I just misread 'almost all' for 'all' when adding the final remark.

Yes, Erdos even mentions that he thinks any $A$ is probably not true in this sufficiently large setting. GPT-5.4 can pretty quickly produce a counterexample with $A$ sparse enough. Also $k=3$ is indeed quite difficult, but there seems to be a way. The Ford–Green–Konyagin–Tao machinery already proves the prime-only statement on arbitrarily long overlapping finite windows, and potentially one can globalize those windows into “all sufficiently large integers.” A high-probability version of their $S_2$-random step would very likely finish the argument by a Borel–Cantelli patching over the overlapping windows, but the published proof does not give that. I'm leaving this comment for the future, as this problem might become tractable sooner or later.

There is a gap in Theorem 7, which may be repairable. The issue, near the end of proof, is $\sum_{j \le J} |I_j| \ll x$ is not actually true from $x \in I_J$, because the interval $I_J$ may be large. See last message in this discussion with GPT-5.4 Thinking.
Thank you for checking! Anyway, my note above was mostly related to previous comments below the problem for any $A$ not just primes. With the current statement corrected, you can disregard it.
